questions.query: If an emergency defect occurs under the warranty (e.g., burst pipe), what is the required notification channel and consequence of delay?

runs.text:
## Summary

If an emergency defect occurs under the warranty, such as a burst pipe, the Buyer/Owner is required to immediately notify the Builder by telephone using the emergency number provided. Only emergency reports are accepted by phone. Failure to promptly notify the Builder of an emergency relieves the Builder of all liability for replacement, repair, and all other damages related to the defect [1][2].

## Key Points

- The Buyer/Owner must immediately telephone the Builder in the event of an emergency defect (e.g., burst pipe) [1].
- Only emergency reports will be taken by phone; all other defects must be reported in writing by certified mail [1][2].
- Failure to promptly notify the Builder of an emergency by phone relieves the Builder of all liability for replacement, repair, and all other damages [2].
- The emergency number provided for such notifications is (208) 555-2311 [1].

evidence_path_sig: failure to notify Builder of emergency
one (1) year or sixty (60) day warranty period

questions.query: In the property management agreement, what happens to confirmed reservations if the agreement is terminated or the property is sold?

runs.text:
## Summary

If the property management agreement is terminated or the property is sold, the Owner is required to honor all confirmed reservations. Additionally, termination of the agreement does not invalidate any existing rental agreements made by the Agent on the Owner's behalf. The Owner remains responsible for completing all obligations or expenses incurred under the agreement by the Agent, even after termination [1][2].

## Key Points

- Upon termination of the agreement or sale of the property, the Owner must honor all confirmed reservations [1].
- Termination of the agreement does not invalidate any existing rental agreements made by the Agent on the Owner's behalf [2].
- The Owner is responsible for completing all obligations or expenses incurred by the Agent under the agreement, even after termination [1].

evidence_path_sig: Does not invalidate existing rental agreements

questions.query: Compare "time windows" across the set: list all explicit day-based timeframes.

runs.text:
## Summary

Across the provided documents, explicit day-based timeframes ("time windows") are as follows: The Builder warrants certain items (doors, windows, electric switches, receptacles, fixtures, caulking, plumbing fixtures, and cabinet work) to be free of defects in materials or workmanship for a period of sixty (60) days from the date of final settlement or first occupancy, whichever comes first [1]. The Builder is obligated to repair or replace these items at no charge within sixty (60) days, unless delays are caused by weather, labor, or material shortages [2]. Additionally, the Builder warrants other components (floors, ceilings, walls, internal structural components, plumbing, heating, electric wiring, and roof) for a period of one (1) year [4]. The terms of the Limited Warranty terminate either one (1) year or sixty (60) days after commencement, as applicable [9]. No other explicit day-based timeframes are stated in the evidence.

## Key Points

- Sixty (60) day warranty period for specified items (doors, windows, electric switches, receptacles, fixtures, caulking, plumbing fixtures, cabinet work) starting from final settlement or first occupancy, whichever is earlier [1].
- Obligation to repair or replace covered items within sixty (60) days, unless delayed by weather, labor, or material shortages [2].
- One (1) year warranty period for floors, ceilings, walls, internal structural components, plumbing, heating, electric wiring, and roof [4].
- The Limited Warranty terminates either one (1) year or sixty (60) days after commencement, depending on the coverage [9].

questions.query: Which documents mention **insurance** and what limits are specified?

runs.text:
## Summary

The PROPERTY MANAGEMENT AGREEMENT mentions insurance requirements. Specifically, it obligates the Owner to provide liability insurance coverage for the property with minimum limits of $300,000 for bodily injury and $25,000 for property damage to others. Additionally, the Agent must be named as an additional insured and furnished with a copy of the insurance policy and a certificate of insurance. No insurance requirements or limits are specified in the BUILDERS LIMITED WARRANTY document; it only references loss or damage from various causes but does not mention insurance coverage or limits [1][3].

## Key Points

- The PROPERTY MANAGEMENT AGREEMENT requires the Owner to provide liability insurance coverage with minimum limits of $300,000 for bodily injury and $25,000 for property damage to others [1].
- The Agent must be named as an additional insured and provided with a copy of the insurance policy and a certificate of insurance [3].
- The BUILDERS LIMITED WARRANTY does not mention insurance or specify any insurance limits [5].

questions.query: In the warranty, explain how the "coverage start" is defined and what must happen before coverage ends.

runs.text:
## Summary

The "coverage start" for the Builder's Limited Warranty is defined as the earlier of two events: the date of final settlement or the date when the Buyer/Owner first occupies the home [1]. The coverage ends either one (1) year or sixty (60) days after commencement, as applicable, unless otherwise provided in the warranty [1]. Before coverage ends, the Buyer/Owner must send a written letter describing any defect to the Builder by certified mail, return receipt requested, within the applicable warranty period (one year or sixty days) [2]. Additionally, if the Buyer/Owner sells the home or moves out, the warranty automatically terminates [3]. Claims not made in writing within the specified period are not covered, regardless of discoverability [5].

## Key Points

- Coverage starts on the earlier of the date of final settlement or the date when the Buyer/Owner first occupies the home [1].
- Coverage ends one (1) year or sixty (60) days after commencement, as applicable, unless otherwise specified [1].
- Before coverage ends, the Buyer/Owner must submit a written claim describing the defect to the Builder by certified mail, return receipt requested, within the warranty period [2].
- The Buyer/Owner must specify times they will be at home for scheduling service calls [2].
- If the Buyer/Owner sells the home or moves out, the warranty automatically terminates [3].
- Claims not made in writing within the one (1) year or sixty (60) day period are not covered, regardless of discoverability [5].

Before coverage ends, the Buyer/Owner must send a written letter describing any defect to the Builder by certified mail, return receipt requested, within the applicable warranty period (one year or sixty days) [2]. Additionally, if the Buyer/Owner sells the home or moves out, the warranty automatically terminates [3]. Claims not made in writing within the specified period are not covered, regardless of discoverability [5].

questions.query: Do the purchase contract total price and the invoice total match? If so, what is that amount?

runs.text:
## Summary

Yes, the purchase contract total price and the invoice total match. Both documents state the total amount as $29,900.00. The purchase contract specifies a total contract price of $29,900.00, payable in three installments: $20,000.00 upon signing, $7,000.00 upon delivery, and $2,900.00 upon completion [3]. The invoice lists a subtotal, total, and amount due of $29,900.00, with no tax applied [1]. The invoice itemizes the products and services provided, and their combined total equals $29,900.00 [1].

## Key Points

- The purchase contract total price is $29,900.00, payable in three installments: $20,000.00 upon signing, $7,000.00 upon delivery, and $2,900.00 upon completion [3].
- The invoice subtotal, total, and amount due are all $29,900.00, with no tax applied [1].
- The invoice itemizes products and services whose combined total is $29,900.00 [1].
- Both documents explicitly state the same total price: $29,900.00 [1][3].

questions.query: Compare the "fees" concepts: which doc has a percentage-based fee structure and which has fixed installment payments?

runs.text:
## Summary

The "fees" concepts in the two documents differ in structure. The Property Management Agreement uses a percentage-based fee structure for several services, including a 25% commission on gross revenues for short-term/vacation rentals and a 10% commission on gross revenues for long-term leases, as well as a 10% fee on the total charge for repairs, upgrades, and renovations. It also includes some fixed fees, such as a $35/hour charge for scheduling or escorting contractors, a $75/month advertising charge, a $50/month administration fee, and a $250 non-refundable start-up fee. In contrast, the Purchase Contract specifies fixed installment payments for the total contract price of $29,900.00, divided into three installments: $20,000.00 upon signing, $7,000.00 upon delivery, and $2,900.00 upon completion [1][2][3][4][5][6][7].

## Key Points

- The Property Management Agreement includes percentage-based fees: 25% of gross revenues for short-term/vacation rentals, 10% of gross revenues for long-term leases, and 10% on the total charge for repairs, upgrades, and renovations [1][6].
- The Property Management Agreement also includes fixed fees: $35/hour for scheduling/escorting contractors, $75/month for advertising, $50/month for administration, and a $250 non-refundable start-up fee [1][2][3].
- The Purchase Contract uses a fixed installment payment structure: $20,000.00 upon signing, $7,000.00 upon delivery, and $2,900.00 upon completion, totaling $29,900.00 [5].
- The Property Management Agreement also specifies that the Hawaii Gross Excise tax (currently 4.712%) will be charged on all fees, commissions, and charges [3].

full_response.response:
## Summary

The "fees" concepts in the two documents differ in structure. The Property Management Agreement uses a percentage-based fee structure for several services, including a 25% commission on gross revenues for short-term/vacation rentals and a 10% commission on gross revenues for long-term leases, as well as a 10% fee on the total charge for repairs, upgrades, and renovations. It also includes some fixed fees, such as a $35/hour charge for scheduling or escorting contractors, a $75/month advertising charge, a $50/month administration fee, and a $250 non-refundable start-up fee. In contrast, the Purchase Contract specifies fixed installment payments for the total contract price of $29,900.00, divided into three installments: $20,000.00 upon signing, $7,000.00 upon delivery, and $2,900.00 upon completion [1][2][3][4][5][6][7].

## Key Points

- The Property Management Agreement includes percentage-based fees: 25% of gross revenues for short-term/vacation rentals, 10% of gross revenues for long-term leases, and 10% on the total charge for repairs, upgrades, and renovations [1][6].
- The Property Management Agreement also includes fixed fees: $35/hour for scheduling/escorting contractors, $75/month for advertising, $50/month for administration, and a $250 non-refundable start-up fee [1][2][3].
- The Purchase Contract uses a fixed installment payment structure: $20,000.00 upon signing, $7,000.00 upon delivery, and $2,900.00 upon completion, totaling $29,900.00 [5].
- The Property Management Agreement also specifies that the Hawaii Gross Excise tax (currently 4.712%) will be charged on all fees, commissions, and charges [3].

questions.query: List the three different "risk allocation" statements across the set (risk of loss, liability limitations, non-transferability).

runs.text:
## Summary

The documents contain three distinct "risk allocation" statements: (1) risk of loss is allocated to the contractor until delivery and to the customer thereafter in the purchase contract; (2) liability limitations are set in the property management agreement, which limits the agent's liability except in cases of gross negligence or willful misconduct and requires the owner to indemnify the agent; (3) non-transferability is addressed in both the purchase contract, which restricts assignment without written consent, and the builders limited warranty, which is non-transferable and terminates if the first purchaser sells or moves out.

## Key Points

- Risk of loss: The contractor holds risk until delivery, after which the customer assumes the risk [1].
- Liability limitations: The property management agreement limits the agent's liability for loss or injury on the property except for cases of gross negligence or willful misconduct, and requires the owner to indemnify the agent for such claims [4].
- Non-transferability: The purchase contract prohibits assignment without the other party's written consent [3], and the builders limited warranty is non-transferable, terminating if the first purchaser sells or moves out [2].
